name: Vicki Schmidt
occupation: Kansas Insurance Commissioner; former pharmacist and state senator
education: University of Kansas School of Pharmacy (Class of 1978); Wichita South High School
family: Married to Dr. Michael J. Schmidt, MD (orthopedic surgeon) for over 50 years; two sons; four grandchildren
whoTheyAre:
Vicki Schmidt is a 70-year-old Republican running for governor of Kansas in 2026. She was born and raised in Wichita, where she met her husband Mike at Wichita South High School. Their families lived two blocks apart. She graduated from the University of Kansas School of Pharmacy in 1978, and the couple married in 1974. They have lived in Topeka for over 40 years, where they raised their two sons. She has four grandchildren.

Schmidt spent more than 40 years as a working pharmacist. Early in her career, while working as a state employee on the Kansas Medical Assistance Program, she discovered errors that were costing the state millions of dollars. She put together a plan that recouped more than $391 million for Kansas. That work launched her into public life. She served 14 years in the Kansas Senate representing District 20, rising to Assistant Majority Leader and chairing the Public Health and Welfare Committee. In 2018 she was elected Kansas Insurance Commissioner, winning with nearly 63% of the vote. She was re-elected in 2022 with a similar margin. She is the first pharmacist to hold statewide elected office in Kansas.

In May 2023, Schmidt was diagnosed with breast cancer following a routine mammogram. She went public with her diagnosis, saying the decision made her "very vulnerable," and was declared cancer-free by September 2023. She has since used that experience to advocate for expanded breast cancer screening coverage. Her husband Mike is an orthopedic surgeon with over 46 years of experience practicing in Topeka.
theirRecord:
As a state senator, Schmidt championed health care legislation. She helped pass a law requiring insurance coverage for the diagnosis and treatment of autism in children, and she helped secure seed funding for the University of Kansas Cancer Center, which went on to earn prestigious National Cancer Institute comprehensive designation. She served on the Financial Institutions and Insurance Committee for 12 years and chaired the Public Health and Welfare Committee for six years.

As Insurance Commissioner, Schmidt's office has recovered more than $202.7 million for Kansas families in insurance disputes and lowered the cost of doing business by over $96 million. In 2024, she championed the Protect Vulnerable Adults from Financial Exploitation Act, which has prevented nearly $5 million from going to scammers targeting seniors. She rose to chair of the National Association of Insurance Commissioners Midwest Zone, overseeing 13 states. She has also called for reform of pharmacy benefit managers, drawing on her pharmacy background.

Schmidt's record has drawn significant criticism from the right on abortion. She was the only Republican in the Kansas Senate to vote against both a 2011 bill blocking taxpayer-funded abortions and the 2015 Unborn Child Protection from Dismemberment Abortion Act. Kansans for Life PAC has said she has "the worst record on life of any Republican to ever seek office in Kansas" and is actively opposing her candidacy. She also voted for the 2012 Brownback tax cuts, which she now says she regrets. She has called that vote a lesson in not trusting the legislative process. In the 2026 session, she championed a breast cancer screening bill (SB 409) that was killed after missing legislative deadlines, with critics saying Senate President Ty Masterson -- a rival for governor -- blocked it for political reasons.
whatYouShouldKnow: Schmidt received the highest vote percentage of any statewide candidate in both 2018 and 2022. In 2022, she outperformed every other Republican on the ticket and won all four congressional districts, including one that elected a Democrat to Congress.

Kansans for Life PAC is actively opposing her, calling her Senate abortion votes "the worst record on life of any Republican to ever seek office in Kansas." She was the only Republican senator to vote against both a taxpayer-funded abortion restriction and the dismemberment abortion ban.
She survived the 2012 Brownback-era Republican purge of moderate senators by only 160 votes when Koch Industries and the Kansas Chamber recruited a conservative challenger against her.
She boycotted the January 2026 Republican gubernatorial debate because the Kansas GOP required candidates to pay $10,000 to participate, calling it a system that "picks winners and losers."

Her breast cancer screening bill (SB 409) was killed in the 2026 legislative session. Democrats and media reports attributed its death to political retaliation by Senate President Ty Masterson, a rival gubernatorial candidate. Republican leadership blamed pushback from the business community.
She was diagnosed with breast cancer in May 2023 and declared cancer-free by September 2023. She has used her experience to advocate for eliminating out-of-pocket costs on diagnostic breast imaging.
Her campaign reports roughly $980,000 raised with 800+ individual donors and $928,000 cash on hand as of December 2025. She claims the most donor-funded campaign in the Republican primary field.
She says she regrets voting for the 2012 Brownback tax cuts, which led to a state revenue crisis and cuts to education and transportation before being repealed in 2017.
whereTheyWorship:
Schmidt's campaign materials reference an upbringing focused on "hard work, honesty, and faith," and her campaign announcement video includes a scene at a church where someone asks if she is running for governor. However, no specific church, parish, or denomination has been publicly identified in any campaign material, interview, official biography, or news coverage reviewed in this investigation.

Among the Republican candidates for governor, Schmidt has the least visible public faith profile. She does not appear to use faith as a front-and-center part of her political identity. Given her 40-plus years in Topeka, she may attend a mainline Protestant congregation, but this is unconfirmed.

church.details: No specific church affiliation was found in any public source. Campaign materials reference faith in general terms but do not name a church or denomination.

quotes.text: It's time Kansans have a governor that works as hard as they do.

Throughout my personal and professional career, I have a track record of serving Kansans and actually getting things done. If elected governor, my top priority is the people of Kansas and making their lives better.

It is a vote I regret. [...] The GOP strategy in the Senate was to place the tax bill in the hands of a House-Senate negotiating committee so a more balanced reform plan could be drafted. Instead of revisions, the House concurred with the Senate and Brownback signed the bill into law. We know how that all turned out. I sincerely regret that I trusted the process to move that along, but I will tell you that I learned a big lesson that day. You can't always trust the process.

The (U.S.) Supreme Court did the right thing, in my opinion, of bringing that back to the states and letting the states decide. Like most Kansans, I don't believe in abortion on demand, like some of the far left do. ... But I do believe in reasonable exceptions, and I believe that that is where most Kansans are.

Under this agreement, the party picks winners and losers. President Trump has brought new voters to the party and we need to be encouraging good folks to run for office as Republicans, not creating road blocks and challenges that discourage them.

When I was in pharmacy school, I remember hearing about patients coming in for their medication and being grumpy, and how you don't know what's happened to them. They may have had a bad diagnosis, or not heard from their kids in a while, or had a fight with a loved one. What I found was that just by asking a simple question like 'How's your day going?' you could find out all kinds of things and even help people.
We're building a campaign the right way -- with broad grassroots support, careful stewardship of resources and a clear path forward.

Today, I'm calling on Alex Dwyer & William Hendrix to step down from their positions with the @KansasYRs. Their actions are disgusting and disgraceful and they tarnish the reputation of Kansas nationally.
quotes.topic: Kansas Young Republicans racist chat scandal

campaignFinance.narrative: Schmidt's 2025 year-end filing with the Kansas Ethics Commission shows roughly $980,000 raised, $52,211 spent, and $928,161 cash on hand. Her campaign highlights having more than 800 individual donors, with contributions ranging from $1 to $4,000. About 57% of donations came from Kansas residents. She also loaned her campaign $81,500 of her own money, which is relatively modest compared to some self-funded rivals in the race. Scott Schwab raised $1.3 million and Philip Sarnecki received about $900,000 in the same period.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: The full 104-page itemized donor schedule was only partially parsed. The donors listed here are a sample from the publicly accessible filing, not the complete list.
